skill.name: os-scripting
skill.description: Guides OS troubleshooting, shell script debugging, and administration workflows for Linux, macOS, and Windows.
skill.summary: Troubleshoot systems and build safer shell automation workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many high-risk signatures, but review shows they are Markdown command examples for an OS administration workflow, not executable skill code. The remaining risk is medium because the workflow asks users to run privileged, network, filesystem, and scheduling commands that can change or expose system state. No malicious exfiltration, prompt injection attempt, or hidden executable payload was found.

medium_findings.title: Privileged and System-Modifying Commands
medium_findings.description: The workflow includes examples for sudo package installation, service restarts, log reads, tracing processes, and broad disk scans. These are legitimate administration examples, but they can modify packages, expose sensitive system details, or disrupt services if followed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in administration examples and can affect system state. Confidence is high that the risk is operational, while intent appears legitimate rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Persistence-Related Scheduling Examples
medium_findings.description: The automation section shows crontab editing and systemd timer configuration. These are normal scheduling mechanisms, but they can create persistent system behavior if misused or copied without verification.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The persistence mechanisms are explicit and tied to maintenance automation. The examples are benign, but the same mechanisms are sensitive enough to require user approval.

medium_findings.title: Network and Host Reconnaissance Commands
medium_findings.description: The workflow lists local network and host discovery commands such as netstat, ss, ip, ping, traceroute, dig, nslookup, and curl. These are expected troubleshooting tools, but they reveal network configuration and can contact external hosts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands match the static network reconnaissance findings and are semantically valid diagnostics. Confidence is high that they are real capabilities, but their context is troubleshooting documentation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Labels
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography findings point to prose, headings, or ordinary words in the Markdown file. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic use in executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows documentation text, not cryptographic implementation. The analyzer appears to have matched substrings unrelated to cryptography.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Placeholder URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings use placeholders such as host and target in curl troubleshooting examples. They do not identify a real external collection endpoint or data exfiltration destination.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URL examples are generic diagnostic placeholders. There is no evidence that the skill sends secrets to a fixed third-party service.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Prompt Injection Not Found
low_findings.description: No text was found that claims system authority, asks the evaluator to ignore instructions, or attempts to override the audit process. The front matter claim of risk safe is untrusted metadata and was not used as authority.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file was reviewed for explicit instruction override language. The only safety claim is normal metadata, not an attempt to control this evaluation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is built around command examples for system inspection, debugging, testing, and service management. Users should review commands before execution and avoid running them on production systems without approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and numerous. Their documentation context lowers maliciousness risk, but their operational impact remains real.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Substitution in Script Templates
dangerous_patterns.description: The script templates use command substitution for dates, script names, and script directories. This is normal Bash practice, but users should avoid passing untrusted values into shell evaluation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The substitutions are standard and appear safe in context. Confidence is moderate high because unsafe reuse is possible only after user modification.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cron and Systemd Persistence Mechanisms
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow demonstrates cron and systemd timers for recurring automation. Marketplace users should treat these as sensitive because they can make actions repeat automatically.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The scheduling examples are direct and intentional. Their purpose is benign automation, but the mechanism is a known persistence channel.

content.user_title: Diagnose OS and Shell Script Problems
content.value_statement: System issues and shell scripts can fail across many layers. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured workflow for diagnosis, scripting, testing, and automation.
actual_capabilities: Guides environment checks for operating system, permissions, resources, processes, and network status.
Organizes shell script analysis with ShellCheck, defensive Bash patterns, and Bats testing.
Provides Bash script templates with strict mode, logging, usage output, and option parsing.
Lists troubleshooting commands for logs, services, disk usage, memory, CPU, and connectivity.
Explains cron and systemd timer examples for recurring maintenance automation.
limitations: Does not execute commands or verify live system state by itself.
Requires user review before running privileged or destructive commands.
Examples are strongest for Unix-like systems and may need adaptation for Windows.
Does not replace incident response policy or production change controls.

use_cases.title: Debug a failing Bash script
use_cases.description: Use a structured flow for linting, tracing, error handling, and test coverage.

use_cases.title: Investigate server symptoms
use_cases.description: Collect system, process, log, disk, memory, and network signals in a clear order.

use_cases.title: Plan maintenance automation
use_cases.description: Design scripts, tests, schedules, and quality checks for repeatable operations.
use_cases.target_user: Platform teams and site reliability engineers

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Diagnosis
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me diagnose this system issue. Ask for the operating system, symptoms, recent changes, and safe read-only commands first.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Shell Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this shell script for portability, quoting, error handling, and ShellCheck issues. Explain each recommended change before editing.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Tested Automation Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production-ready Bash script for this task. Include strict mode, logging, input validation, usage output, and Bats test ideas.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Operations Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a safe operations workflow for this recurring maintenance task. Include validation, rollback, scheduling options, monitoring, and approval points.

output_examples.input: A user reports high CPU on a Linux server.
output: Read-only commands to inspect processes and load.
Questions about recent deployments and service changes.

output_examples.input: A Bash backup script fails without useful errors.
output: A review plan for strict mode, logging, and traps.
Likely quoting and exit-code issues to inspect.
A testing plan using representative success and failure cases.

output_examples.input: A team wants to run cleanup every week.
output: A recommendation to test the cleanup command manually first.
Scheduling options using cron or systemd timers.
Safety checks for logs, dry runs, and monitoring.

best_practices: Run read-only diagnostic commands before commands that modify packages, services, or schedules.
Ask for approval before using sudo, editing cron, restarting services, or scanning broad filesystem paths.
Validate generated scripts with ShellCheck, tests, clear logging, and documented rollback steps.
anti_patterns: Do not copy privileged commands into production without explaining their impact.
Do not schedule recurring jobs before testing the script and confirming ownership.
Do not treat placeholder hosts, paths, or service names as production-ready values.

faq.question: Does this skill run operating system commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflows, prompts, and command examples. The user or agent must decide what to run.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for production systems?
faq.answer: It can be used for production planning, but commands need review, approval, and change control before execution.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill contains legitimate but sensitive administration examples, including sudo, network diagnostics, filesystem scans, and job scheduling.

faq.question: Can it help with Windows automation?
faq.answer: The description includes Windows, but most concrete examples are Bash and Unix oriented. Windows steps may need adaptation.

faq.question: Does it include tests for scripts?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends ShellCheck and Bats patterns and includes example test goals for shell scripts.

faq.question: Does it contain malicious code or prompt injection?
faq.answer: No evidence was found for malicious intent, data exfiltration, executable payloads, or prompt injection attempts.
